title: A Life of Philip K. Dick: The Man Who Remembered the Future
author: Anthony Peake
card_summary: A biographical work on Philip K. Dick by Anthony Peake, subtitled 'The Man Who Remembered the Future.' Peake, known for his interest in consciousness and paranormal phenomena, examines Dick's life through the lens of his extraordinary mystical experiences, particularly the events of February 1974. The biography explores Dick's personal history, his prodigious fiction output, his drug use, his marriages, and his ongoing philosophical and theological investigations documented in the Exegesis.

title: Divine Invasions: A Life of Philip K. Dick
author: Lawrence Sutin
card_summary: The full text of Lawrence Sutin's landmark biography of Philip K. Dick, "Divine Invasions: A Life of Philip K. Dick." Dedicated to the author's parents Jack and Rochelle Sutin, the book is described by Sutin as an adventure beyond initial expectations. Sutin credits agent Dorothy Pittman for marketing a proposal that industry insiders considered commercially unviable. At 554 pages, this is the definitive long-form biographical account of Dick's life and remains a foundational reference for PKD scholarship.

title: How to Build an Android: The True Story of Philip K. Dick's Robotic Resurrection
author: David F. Dufty
card_summary: A non-fiction book by David F. Dufty chronicling the creation of the lifelike android replica of Philip K. Dick built by Hanson Robotics and the University of Memphis. The book traces the scientific and artistic collaboration behind the project, covering the researchers involved, the technical challenges of building a convincing humanoid robot, and the android's public appearances and eventual loss. Chapter titles reveal a narrative arc from conception through public debut and the robot's mysterious disappearance.

title: The Divine Madness of Philip K. Dick
author: Kyle Arnold
card_summary: A biographical and psychological study of Philip K. Dick authored by Kyle Arnold and published by Oxford University Press in 2016. The work examines Dick's life, mental states, and creative output, drawing on psychological analysis to illuminate the author's visionary experiences and their relationship to his fiction. Published as part of Oxford's scholarly catalog, this book bridges literary biography and psychological inquiry. A photograph by Tessa B. Dick appears as a frontispiece with permission.

title: The Divine Madness of Philip K. Dick
author: Kyle Arnold
card_summary: A full-length biographical and psychological study of Philip K. Dick by Kyle Arnold, published by Oxford University Press in 2016. The work examines Dick's life through the lens of his mental states, mystical experiences, and the intersection of psychological instability with creative genius. Published by OUP's academic imprint, the book represents a serious scholarly engagement with Dick's biography. A photograph by Tessa B. Dick (Dick's fifth wife) is used with permission as a frontispiece. This is a first edition publication.

title: The Divine Madness of Philip K. Dick
author: Kyle Arnold
card_summary: A psychological biography of Philip K. Dick published by Oxford University Press. At 249 pages, Kyle Arnold examines Dick's life and work through the lens of clinical psychology, exploring the interplay between creativity and mental instability. Features a frontispiece photo by Tessa B. Dick (Philip's last wife). Represents a distinctive approach to Dick biography that takes his psychological experiences seriously as both clinical phenomena and creative catalysts.

title: The Search for Philip K. Dick
author: Anne R. Dick
card_summary: A biographical memoir by Anne R. Dick, one of Philip K. Dick's wives, originally published in 1995 and reissued by Tachyon Publications in a revised edition around 2009-2010. The book draws on Anne's personal experiences living with Dick and interviews with friends and associates to reconstruct his life and character. Interior design by John Coulthart, cover art by Josh Beatman, and photographs by Anne R. Dick. Published with permission from the Philip K. Dick Testamentary Trust for passages from Dick's writings.

title: ArmadilloCon 10 Program Book
author: Various (ArmadilloCon Committee)
card_summary: The official program book for ArmadilloCon 10, Austin's science fiction convention, held October 11-13, 1989 at the Wyndham South hotel. Guests of Honor included author Lewis Shiner, fan writer and File 770 editor Mike Glyer, artist Teddy Harvia (David Thayer), editor Pat LoBrutto of Doubleday Books, and toastmaster Connie Willis. Organized by the Friends of Armadillo fan group (FACT) based in Austin, Texas. The 36-page program book documents the convention's schedule, guests, and activities, and includes information about advance membership for the following year's event.

title: Journey Planet Issue 16: The Philip K. Dick Issue
author: Peter Young, James Bacon, Chris Garcia, and various contributors
card_summary: Journey Planet issue 16, subtitled 'Do Androids Dream of Electric Fanzines?', is a fanzine dedicated entirely to Philip K. Dick. Edited by Peter Young, James Bacon, and Chris Garcia, it features essays, memoir pieces, reviews, and reprinted fiction. Contents include a review of Tessa B. Dick's 'The Owl in Daylight', a piece on 'A Scanner Darkly', an interview with Blade Runner collector Geoff Hutchins, a Chris Moore retrospective, a reprint of PKD's short story 'The Eyes Have It', a letter from Ted White, and memorial tributes by Tim Powers and others. 46 pages.

title: Me and PKD
author: Unknown (fan author)
card_summary: A personal fan essay by an unnamed author describing their passionate engagement with Philip K. Dick's work. The author, a visual artist and parent who studied at Western Illinois University, reflects on how their 'obsession' with PKD transcends ordinary fandom and intersects with their art practice and personal identity. Written in an informal, confessional register, the piece traces the author's discovery of Dick and their aspiration to become an authority on his work. Representative of grassroots PKD fan culture and the personal impact of Dick's writing on readers. 5 pages.

title: Niekas Issue 11
author: Ed Meskys, Felice Rolfe, and various contributors
card_summary: Issue 11 of Niekas, a science fiction fanzine published quarterly to meet the activity requirements of the N'APA (National Amateur Press Association) amateur press alliance. Edited by Ed Meskys and Felice Rolfe, with mail addresses in Berkeley and Livermore, California. Niekas was a prominent and long-running SF fanzine of the 1960s and 1970s that frequently featured discussion of Philip K. Dick's work alongside broader SF criticism, letters, and fan commentary. This issue includes editorial notes about the editors potentially relocating from the Bay Area. 77 pages.

title: Niekas Issue 28
author: Ed Meskys and various contributors
card_summary: Issue 28 of Niekas, a science fiction and fantasy fanzine. This issue features extensive artwork from contributors including Diana Paxson, Terry Jeeves, Eddie Jones, and others, alongside fiction and critical content. Niekas was a Hugo Award-winning fanzine known for in-depth SF commentary and maintained a British agent (Roger Waddington) indicating international distribution. The archive likely holds this issue due to PKD-related content within its pages. Contributors and artists are credited across 72 pages of science fiction and fantasy discussion, fiction, and illustration.

title: PKD Otaku #22
author: Various contributors; edited by Marc Oberst and Patrick Clark
card_summary: Issue 22 of PKD Otaku, a Philip K. Dick fan publication, dated May 14, 2011. The editors credit contributors jami morgan, Frank Bertrand, and John Fairchild as providing the bulk of the content for this issue, with additional contributions from the regular editors Marc and Patrick. The editorial expresses the publication's longstanding aim to present diverse voices and perspectives on Philip K. Dick, welcoming insights, arguments, close readings, personal experiences, and discoveries. The issue reflects the active fan scholarship community surrounding Dick's life and work.

title: Ray Nelson: A Tale of Two Bookstores
author: Ray Nelson
card_summary: A piece authored by or about Ray Nelson (Radell Faraday Nelson), science fiction writer and close friend and collaborator of Philip K. Dick. Nelson and Dick co-authored the novel The Ganymede Takeover (1967) and were longtime associates in the Bay Area SF community. 'A Tale of Two Bookstores' appears to be a memoir or reminiscence connected to the SF fan and literary community. Four pages.

title: SF Commentary, Issue 1
author: Bruce Gillespie (ed.)
card_summary: The first issue of SF Commentary, an Australian science fiction fanzine edited by Bruce Gillespie, incorporating Australian Science Fiction Review. The editorial describes the magazine's vision of science and speculative fiction as encompassing private myths, extrapolative dreams, and scientific and philosophical ideas. This long-running fanzine became one of the most respected critical SF publications in the world and frequently featured content related to Philip K. Dick over its many decades of publication.

title: SF Commentary, Issue 17
author: Bruce Gillespie (ed.)
card_summary: Issue 17 of SF Commentary, the Australian critical SF fanzine edited by Bruce Gillespie. This issue features an extensive checklist of authors and works mentioned, including Poul Anderson, Isaac Asimov, J.G. Ballard, Ray Bradbury, Arthur C. Clarke, and many others. The checklist format with page-number references indicates a substantial critical and review-oriented issue covering a wide range of science fiction authors and publications, including references to the Australian Science Fiction Yearbook and various fanzines such as Outworlds and Warhoon.

title: SF Commentary, Issue 31 (Philip Dick in Vancouver)
author: Bruce Gillespie (ed.)
card_summary: Issue 31 of SF Commentary, the Australian critical SF fanzine edited by Bruce Gillespie. This issue is notable for featuring coverage of Philip Dick in Vancouver and Ivan Illich in Melbourne. The issue includes a stop-press note about the 1975 World Science Fiction Convention bid and a checklist for the preceding issue (SFC 30), referencing works and authors including Brian Aldiss, Ray Bradbury, and Edgar Rice Burroughs. The Dick-in-Vancouver content likely relates to his 1972 speech 'The Android and the Human' delivered at the University of British Columbia.

title: SF Commentary, Issue 4
author: Bruce Gillespie (ed.)
card_summary: Issue 4 of SF Commentary, dated July 1969, priced at $3.00 for 9 issues, edited by Bruce Gillespie and incorporating the Australian Science Fiction Review. The issue includes a section titled 'Raison d'Etre' by the editor and a letters/review section called 'Invisible Whistling Bunyips' featuring contributions from notable SF figures including Peter Darling, George Turner, Brian Aldiss, Harry Harrison, Franz Rottensteiner, and many others. This issue is an early number of what became a landmark Australian SF critical fanzine.

title: Scratch Pad 44: The Philip K. Dick Special
author: Bruce Gillespie
card_summary: Scratch Pad 44 is an extract from The Great Cosmic Donut of Life No. 30, a personal fanzine by Australian SF critic Bruce Gillespie, produced for the June 2001 mailing of Acnestis. This issue is a Philip K. Dick special, featuring an interview with Gillespie by Frank Bertrand titled 'My Life and Philip K. Dick,' an essay 'Philip K. Dick: The Real Thing,' and a survey of 'The Non-Science Fiction Novels of Philip K. Dick (1928-82).' The front cover features artwork by Ditmar (Dick Jenssen) depicting Dune Messiah.

title: Simulacrum Meltdown, Issue 3
author: Unknown (fan editor)
card_summary: Simulacrum Meltdown Issue 3, dated October 2001, is a Philip K. Dick fan publication. This issue continues an essay on Dick's novel Dr. Futurity from the previous issue, includes a section on Phil Dick and drugs, and discusses the novel Time Out of Joint. The editor opens with a quote about Horselover Fat from VALIS and notes the publication's irregular schedule. The zine engages seriously with PKD's bibliography and biography, offering close readings of individual novels and thematic essays on recurring subjects in Dick's life and work.

title: The Break-In: The Timothy Leary Connection
author: Don Dulchinos
card_summary: A piece prepared by Don Dulchinos for the PKD Festival in Fort Morgan, Colorado, Summer 2024, examining the connection between Philip K. Dick and Timothy Leary via the famous 1971 break-in at Dick's home. Includes a Facebook PKD Fan Group exchange from April 2021 discussing the leather jacket Winona Ryder wore while filming A Scanner Darkly, which allegedly once belonged to PKD, and related stories about Dick's connection to psychedelic culture. Two pages.

title: The Empire Never Ended - But It Did Get a Makeover
author: Don Dulchinos
card_summary: A four-page essay prepared by Don Dulchinos for the PKD Festival in Fort Morgan, Colorado, Summer 2024, exploring Philip K. Dick's Gnostic concept that 'the (Roman) Empire never ended,' a theme central to VALIS and The Exegesis. Dulchinos draws parallels between Dick's premise in The Man in the High Castle (that World War 2 never ended) and PKD's later metaphysical claim, comparing it to Thomas Pynchon's Gravity's Rainbow and examining its contemporary geopolitical resonance with American, Chinese, Saudi, and Russian imperial interpenetration.

title: The Palm Tree Garden of Philip K. Dick
author: Paul Rydeen (ed.)
card_summary: A collection of essays about Philip K. Dick edited by Paul Rydeen, originally compiled in 1999 with an electronic version issued in 2009, featuring cover art by Randy Moore. The anthology includes contributions from multiple authors on eclectic PKD-related topics: the Ganymedean Slime Mold Codex by Dave Hyde, an introduction to PKD by Joel Margot, essays on the Black Knight Satellite and VALIS, PKD and the Illuminati by Jim Keith, Philip K. Dick and the Kabbalah by Paul Rydeen, and pieces on 2-3-74, cyberpunk connections, and other themes. Individual contributions retain their own copyrights.

title: The Phil Dick-manack, Issue 33,132 (September 2019)
author: Frank T. Hollander (editor)
card_summary: Issue 33,132 (dated September 1, 2019) of The Phil Dick-manack, a fan sourcebook for Philip K. Dick collectors, edited by Frank T. Hollander. This issue is dedicated to the memory of Anthony Boucher. Content is published under a Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International License, with copyrighted material quoted under fair use provisions for commentary, criticism, research, and scholarship. At 170 pages, it is a substantial fan reference publication covering PKD-related collecting and scholarship.

title: Vector 67/68 (Spring 1974)
author: Malcolm Edwards (editor); Philip K. Dick, Ursula Le Guin, Gene Wolfe, Brian Aldiss, and others
card_summary: A double issue (67/68) of Vector, the journal of the British Science Fiction Association, published in Spring 1974 and edited by Malcolm Edwards. This issue features contributions from Philip K. Dick ('Letters from Amerika'), Ursula Le Guin, Gene Wolfe, Brian Aldiss, Michael G. Coney, Peter Nicholls, Brian Stableford, and Philip Strick, among others. PKD's 'Letters from Amerika' is of particular archival interest as a direct contribution by PKD to the British SF community. The issue also includes a Tolkien symposium and book reviews.

title: WCSFAzine #6 (February 2008)
author: West Coast Science Fiction Association
card_summary: Issue 6 (February 2008, Volume 2, Number 2) of WCSFAzine, the monthly electronic fanzine of the West Coast Science Fiction Association (WCSFA), a registered society founded in 1993 dedicated to promoting science fiction and sponsoring the annual VCon convention. This issue features on its cover an image of Light #122 (November 1942), described as the most popular Canadian sci-fi fanzine of the 1940s, edited by Leslie A. Croutch. The publication provides a window into fan community activity and the broader North American science fiction fan culture that intersected with Philip K. Dick's readership.

title: French-Language PKD Documentary or Interview Transcript
author: Unknown; includes Daniel Rich
card_summary: A French-language document, likely a transcript of a documentary or interview program about Philip K. Dick. The text discusses photographs of Dick with cats, his tomb, a late photograph taken with Ridley Scott during the editing of Blade Runner, and a pet shop where Dick reportedly bought horse meat for personal consumption when financially destitute. It references publisher Daniel Rich, who holds rare materials including an exceptional recorded interview with PKD from 1972. A brief English-language fragment attributed to Dick is also present.

title: PKD Magazine Interviews: Compiled Transcripts and Analysis
author: Various; compiled by unknown editor
card_summary: A 167-page compilation of Philip K. Dick's magazine interviews and related scholarly analysis. The document opens with an examination of 'Three Sci-Fi Authors View the Future,' published in Scholastic Voice on January 17, 1974, tracing its bibliographic history through Levack's bibliography, the Stephensen-Payne and Benson updated bibliography, and Frank Bertrand's research. The compiler investigates a letter from Dick to Ursula K. Le Guin (February 2, 1974) in which Dick describes being interviewed by Voice, and critically interrogates whether the piece qualifies as a nonfiction article or an interview.

title: Philip K. Dick: In His Own Words
author: Gregg Rickman
card_summary: Book-length collection of interviews with Philip K. Dick conducted by Gregg Rickman, with a foreword by Roger Zelazny. Published by Fragments West/Valentine Press in Long Beach, CA. Contains extended biographical interviews organized into chapters covering Dick's life, themes, and philosophical views, plus a critical essay by Rickman on Dick's novels as a search for caritas.

title: Philip K. Dick: The Last Interview and Other Conversations
author: Philip K. Dick (interviews compiled by Melville House Publishing)
card_summary: A curated collection of interview transcripts with Philip K. Dick, published by Melville House in 2015. Includes 'Local Kid Makes Good' from the Oakland Tribune (1955), 'The Acid Truth' from Vertex (1974) by Arthur Byron Cover, 'Even Paranoids Have Enemies' by Cindy Lee Berryhill, 'Anticipating the Future' by D. Scott Apel (1987), 'Killing the Rat' by Charles Platt (originally in Dream Makers, 1980), and a conversation on Blade Runner and Hollywood. Spans nearly three decades of Dick's public interviews and conversations.

title: The Mainstream That Through the Ghetto Flows: An Interview with Philip K. Dick
author: Philip K. Dick (interviewee)
card_summary: An interview with Philip K. Dick published in The Missouri Review (Vol. 7, No. 2, 1984). The title references Dick's lifelong tension between science fiction (the 'ghetto') and mainstream literary ambitions. One of the most substantial literary interviews Dick gave, published posthumously. 23 pages.

title: A Dark-Haired Girl & PKD: Letters from the Heart, the Spleen, and the Funnybone
author: Philip K. Dick; Linda Levy; compiled by Linda Castellani
card_summary: A collection of correspondence between Philip K. Dick and Linda Levy, compiled and edited by Linda Castellani (copyright 2008). The volume is subtitled 'Letters from the Heart, the Spleen, and the Funnybone,' reflecting the emotional and humorous range of the exchange. Organized into sections covering a preface and three labeled sides attributed to PKD, suggesting a structured editorial arrangement of letters across different phases or themes of the correspondence.

title: Dick Letter on Writing
author: Philip K. Dick
card_summary: A letter by Philip K. Dick concerning the craft or practice of writing. The recipient and precise date are unknown. Five pages. Likely a significant primary-source document for understanding Dick's views on his own literary practice.

title: PKD Letter (September, undated)
author: Philip K. Dick
card_summary: A letter by Philip K. Dick, apparently written in September of an unspecified year. PKD was a prolific correspondent and his letters are significant primary sources documenting his thoughts on writing, philosophy, personal life, and literary relationships. The specific recipient and year are not determinable.

title: PKD Signed Letters (Copies)
author: Philip K. Dick
card_summary: Copies of signed letters by Philip K. Dick. These are reproductions of original signed correspondence, likely containing a small number of individual letters with high-quality reproductions of the originals.

title: Selected Letters of Philip K. Dick, 1975-1976
author: Philip K. Dick
card_summary: A collection of Philip K. Dick's correspondence from 1975 and 1976, edited by Don Herron with an introduction by Tim Powers, published by Underwood-Miller in 1992. The volume continues the Underwood-Miller selected letters series covering Dick's post-2-3-74 years, when he was deeply engaged in writing the Exegesis and producing major late novels. Powers, a close personal friend of Dick's during this period, provides an intimate introduction based on firsthand knowledge of Dick's life and thinking.

title: Statement by Stanislaw Lem Regarding Philip K. Dick
author: Stanislaw Lem
card_summary: A formal public statement by Polish science fiction author Stanislaw Lem responding to allegations made by Philip K. Dick, including accusations that Lem stole royalties from the Polish edition of Dick's novel UBIK. Lem expresses his general reluctance to see many of Dick's letters published, citing their damaging character and purely psychological rather than literary interest. He firmly denies the financial allegation, noting his admiration for UBIK, and addresses Dick's reported denunciations to law enforcement. A significant document in the troubled Dick-Lem correspondence and relationship.

title: The Selected Letters of Philip K. Dick, Volume 1: 1938–1971
author: Philip K. Dick (edited by Don Herron; introduction by James Blaylock)
card_summary: The first volume of Philip K. Dick's selected correspondence, covering letters written between 1938 and 1971, published by Underwood Books of Grass Valley, California in 1996. The collection is introduced by James Blaylock and was published by arrangement with The Estate of Philip K. Dick. Available in both trade and slipcased editions. The volume provides primary source insight into Dick's personal, professional, and intellectual development across more than three decades, from his early years through his mature period as a science fiction author.

title: The Selected Letters of Philip K. Dick: 1972-1973
author: Philip K. Dick
card_summary: The volume of Dick's selected letters covering 1972-1973, published by Underwood Books. At 423 pages, the largest volume in the series, covering a tumultuous period that includes Dick's famous trip to Vancouver for the SF convention where he delivered "The Android and the Human," his brief sojourn in Canada, and his eventual relocation to Orange County. The book opens with a comprehensive bibliography of Dick's published works from 1955 through the mid-1960s.

title: The Selected Letters of Philip K. Dick: 1977-1979
author: Philip K. Dick (edited by Don Herron, introduction by Robert Anton Wilson)
card_summary: The volume of Dick's selected letters covering 1977-1979, edited by Don Herron with an introduction by Robert Anton Wilson. Published by Underwood-Miller (Novato, California / Lancaster, Pennsylvania) in 1993. ISBN 0-88733-120-3 (trade), 0-88733-121-1 (slipcased). At 300 pages, this volume covers a critical period in Dick's intellectual and creative life, as he processed the 2-3-74 mystical experiences and worked on the Exegesis. Frontispiece photo by Nicole Panter (1980).

title: The Selected Letters of Philip K. Dick: 1980-1982
author: Philip K. Dick
card_summary: The final volume of Dick's selected letters, covering 1980-1982, the last years of his life. ISBN 1-887424-26-1. At 344 pages, the volume opens with a passage describing Dick's second theophany in November (likely 1980), which he considered the most treasured moment of his life. Dick describes standing in his kitchen with his friend Ray Torrence (mentioned in the story "Frozen Journey") at about eleven a.m. This volume documents Dick's final creative and spiritual period.

title: Daily Independent Journal — April 21, 1962 (Page 1)
author: Daily Independent Journal
card_summary: A clipped front page from the Daily Independent Journal (San Rafael, California), dated Saturday, April 21, 1962. As a front-page clipping from 1962, this item is likely preserved as a record of local news relevant to Philip K. Dick's life and surroundings during his period of residence in the San Rafael area.

title: Daily Independent Journal — April 28, 1962 (Page 8)
author: Daily Independent Journal
card_summary: A clipped page from the Daily Independent Journal (San Rafael, California), dated Saturday, April 28, 1962, page 8. Likely contains Philip K. Dick or events in his local environment during 1962.

title: Daily Independent Journal — April 4, 1963 (Page 30)
author: Daily Independent Journal
card_summary: A clipped page from the Daily Independent Journal (San Rafael, California), dated Thursday, April 4, 1963, page 30. Likely contains Philip K. Dick or local affairs during his Marin County residence.

title: Daily Independent Journal — February 24, 1959 (Page 2)
author: Daily Independent Journal
card_summary: A clipped page from the Daily Independent Journal (San Rafael, California), dated Tuesday, February 24, 1959, page 2. Likely related to Philip K. Dick or his immediate environment during his San Rafael residence period.

author: Daily Independent Journal
card_summary: A clipped page from the Daily Independent Journal (San Rafael, California), dated Tuesday, July 26, 1960, page 4. Likely related to Philip K. Dick or his local environment during his years living in the Marin County area of California.

author: Daily Independent Journal
card_summary: A clipped page from the Daily Independent Journal (San Rafael, California), dated Friday, March 11, 1960, page 7. Likely related to Philip K. Dick or his local environment during his years living in the San Rafael, Marin County area.

title: Evening Times (Glasgow) — September 15, 2017 (Page M28)
author: Evening Times
card_summary: A clipped page from the Evening Times (Glasgow, Strathclyde, Scotland), dated Friday, September 15, 2017, page M28. Likely contains an article referencing Philip K. Dick, possibly in connection with adaptations or cultural reception of his work in the United Kingdom.

title: Greeley Daily Tribune — July 9, 1932 (Page 3)
author: Greeley Daily Tribune
card_summary: A clipped page from the Greeley Daily Tribune (Greeley, Colorado), dated Saturday, July 9, 1932, page 3. Philip K. Dick was approximately four years old in 1932; this clipping is likely related to his early family history or the Dick family's connection to Greeley, Colorado.

title: Greeley Daily Tribune — September 25, 1934 (Page 5)
author: Greeley Daily Tribune
card_summary: A clipped page from the Greeley Daily Tribune (Greeley, Colorado), dated Tuesday, September 25, 1934, page 5. Philip K. Dick was approximately six years old in 1934; this clipping is likely related to early Dick family history or their connection to the Greeley, Colorado area.

title: LA Weekly, November 22, 1990, Page 6
author: Unknown
card_summary: A single-page clipping from the LA Weekly (Los Angeles, California), dated Thursday, November 22, 1990, page 6. The item is likely a newspaper article or feature related to Philip K. Dick or his literary legacy published in the LA alternative weekly approximately eight years after his death.

title: Le Devoir, December 13, 1975, Page 14
author: Unknown
card_summary: A single-page clipping from Le Devoir (Montreal, Quebec, Canada), a major French-language daily newspaper, dated Saturday, December 13, 1975, page 14. Given the date and source, this is likely a French-language article or review related to Philip K. Dick or his work during the mid-1970s, a period of growing international recognition for the author.

title: National Post, April 28, 2001, Page 37
author: Unknown
card_summary: A single-page clipping from the National Post (Toronto, Ontario, Canada), dated Saturday, April 28, 2001, page 37. Published nearly two decades after Philip K. Dick's death, this clipping likely contains coverage of a PKD-related event, film adaptation, book release, or retrospective piece in a major Canadian national newspaper. Context within the collection suggests relevance to PKD's literary reputation or posthumous influence.

title: New Worlds Article on Philip K. Dick by John Brunner (1966)
author: John Brunner
card_summary: An article about Philip K. Dick written by fellow SF author John Brunner, published in New Worlds magazine in 1966. New Worlds, edited at that time by Michael Moorcock, was a major venue for serious critical engagement with science fiction. Brunner's article represents an early and significant critical appraisal of Dick's work during the period when Dick was producing some of his most important novels.

title: Northeast Star-Review, April 17, 1975, Page 1
author: Unknown
card_summary: A single-page clipping from the Northeast Star-Review (Los Angeles, California), dated Thursday, April 17, 1975, front page. The 1975 date places this during a significant period in PKD's career, shortly after the publication of 'Flow My Tears, the Policeman Said' (1974) and during his prolific essay-writing and public engagement phase. A front-page placement in a local Los Angeles paper suggests a newsworthy local story potentially involving Dick or related subject matter.

title: Oakland Tribune, January 10, 1955, Page 19
author: Unknown
card_summary: A single-page clipping from the Oakland Tribune (Oakland, California), dated Monday, January 10, 1955, page 19. The 1955 date is significant in Dick's early career, when he was actively publishing short stories in science fiction magazines. Oakland was near Dick's Berkeley home base during this period. The clipping likely contains an early notice, review, or mention of Philip K. Dick or his work in a local Bay Area newspaper.

title: Philip K. Dick on Blade Runner (Starlog Magazine)
author: Philip K. Dick
card_summary: A Starlog magazine exclusive feature in which Philip K. Dick discusses the making of the Blade Runner film adaptation of his novel Do Androids Dream of Electric Sheep?. Dick recounts the tortuous history of the screenplay development, including false starts, dead ends, and multiple rewrites. He speaks candidly about his initial shock upon reading the original screenplay, comparing the experience to being condemned to eternal torture, as well as the uneasy alliance between his literary vision and the Hollywood production machine.

title: Point Reyes Light — April 3, 1997 (Page 10)
author: Point Reyes Light Staff
card_summary: A single page from the Point Reyes Light, a weekly community newspaper serving Point Reyes Station, California, dated April 3, 1997, Page 10. This posthumous issue may contain coverage related to PKD's legacy, adaptations, or community items connected to his former residence in the Point Reyes area.

author: Point Reyes Light Staff
card_summary: A single page from the Point Reyes Light, a weekly community newspaper serving Point Reyes Station, California, dated August 10, 1961, Page 1. Philip K. Dick resided in the Point Reyes area during this period, and issues from this paper are archived as contextual documents related to his life and local community.

author: Point Reyes Light Staff

This issue falls in the period following PKD's pivotal 2-3-74 experiences and his return from Vancouver. Issues from this paper are archived as contextual documents relating to the communities and locales significant to PKD's life.
