title: A Maze of Death
author: Philip K. Dick
card_summary: A 1970 novel in which colonists arrive on the mysterious planet Delmak-O, where they begin dying under strange circumstances. The novel layers multiple realities and features Dick's invented theology with entities called the Mentufacturer, the Intercessor, and the Walker-on-Earth. 104 pages.

title: Clans of the Alphane Moon
author: Philip K. Dick
card_summary: A 1964 novel set on a moon colonized as a psychiatric hospital, where patients have organized into clans based on their diagnoses: Manses (manics), Heebs (hebephrenics), Pares (paranoids), Deps (depressives), Skitzes (schizophrenics), and Polys (polymorphous). Dick's satirical exploration of mental illness as social organization. Ace Books EPUB edition.

title: Confessions of a Crap Artist
author: Philip K. Dick
card_summary: Dick's most successful mainstream (non-SF) novel, written in 1959 but not published until 1975. Set in Marin County, California, it follows Jack Isidore, a self-described crap artist who collects pseudo-scientific facts, and his sister Fay. A darkly comic domestic novel revealing Dick's literary range beyond science fiction. Ace Books EPUB edition.

title: Counter-Clock World
author: Philip K. Dick
card_summary: A novel set in a world where time has begun moving backwards: the dead rise from their graves, people grow younger, and food is un-eaten. Originally published in 1967, this Mariner Books/Houghton Mifflin Harcourt edition () preserves one of Dick's most conceptually playful explorations of entropy and temporal reversal.

title: Deus Irae
author: Philip K. Dick and Roger Zelazny
card_summary: A collaborative post-apocalyptic novel by Dick and Roger Zelazny (1976). Set after nuclear devastation, a limbless painter undertakes a pilgrimage to paint the face of the Deus Irae (God of Wrath), the scientist who triggered the apocalypse. Blends Dick's theological concerns with Zelazny's mythological sensibility. 116 pages.

title: Do Androids Dream of Electric Sheep? (Oxford Bookworms Adaptation)
author: Philip K. Dick (adapted by Andy Hopkins and Joc Potter)
card_summary: An adapted/simplified version of Dick's classic novel for the Oxford Bookworms Library (Stage 5, advanced level). Adapted by Andy Hopkins and Joc Potter for English language learners, published by Oxford University Press in 2007. Demonstrates the global educational reach of Dick's most famous novel. 183 pages.

title: Dr. Bloodmoney, or How We Got Along After the Bomb
author: Philip K. Dick
card_summary: A 1965 post-apocalyptic novel set in the San Francisco Bay Area after a nuclear war, following a community of survivors dealing with mutants, a malevolent scientist (Dr. Bluthgeld), and an orbiting astronaut who serves as a DJ. One of Dick's richest ensemble novels. Ace Books 1977 EPUB edition.

title: Eye in the Sky
author: Philip K. Dick
card_summary: A 1957 novel in which a group of people are knocked unconscious by a particle accelerator accident and awaken in successive solipsistic realities, each governed by one person's worldview: a religious fanatic, a paranoid, a prude, and a communist. An early Dick masterwork of subjective reality. Houghton Mifflin Harcourt EPUB edition.

title: Five Great Novels (including A Scanner Darkly)
author: Philip K. Dick
card_summary: An omnibus volume collecting five Philip K. Dick novels, including A Scanner Darkly and likely The Three Stigmata of Palmer Eldritch alongside other major works. A Scanner Darkly, published in 1977, is Dick's semi-autobiographical novel about drug addiction set in a near-future surveillance state.

title: Five Novels of the 1960s and 70s (Library of America)
author: Philip K. Dick
card_summary: The second Library of America volume dedicated to Dick, collecting five novels from his most productive period: likely including Martian Time-Slip, Dr. Bloodmoney, Now Wait for Last Year, Flow My Tears the Policeman Said, and A Scanner Darkly. Dick's inclusion in the Library of America represents his canonical status in American literature.

title: Gather Yourselves Together
author: Philip K. Dick
card_summary: One of Dick's early mainstream (non-SF) novels, written in the early 1950s but not published until 1994. Set in post-war China, the novel follows three Americans left behind at a factory compound as the Communist government takes over. Published posthumously by Houghton Mifflin Harcourt.

title: Lies, Inc.
author: Philip K. Dick
card_summary: An expanded version of Dick's 1966 novel The Unteleported Man, incorporating hallucinatory material removed from the original publication. The novel concerns teleportation to a colony planet and a conspiracy to conceal the truth about conditions there. Published posthumously in 1984. Knopf Doubleday 2007 EPUB edition.

title: Mary and the Giant
author: Philip K. Dick
card_summary: A mainstream novel written in the 1950s and published posthumously in 1987. Set in a small California town, it follows a young woman's relationships with older men, including a Black jazz musician, against the backdrop of 1950s racial tensions and provincial conformity. Reflects Dick's ambitions as a realist literary novelist. Ace Books EPUB edition.

title: Nick and the Glimmung
author: Philip K. Dick
card_summary: Dick's only children's book, written in 1966 but not published until 1988. Nick and his family emigrate to the planet Plowman's Planet, where they encounter the Glimmung, a shape-shifting alien entity. Related to Dick's adult novel Galactic Pot-Healer. Subterranean Press 2009/2013 EPUB edition.

title: Now Wait for Last Year
author: Philip K. Dick
card_summary: A 1966 novel about a drug called JJ-180 that sends users through time, set against an interstellar war between Earth and two alien races. Dr. Eric Sweetscent must navigate a dying marriage, a tyrannical world leader, and the destabilizing effects of temporal displacement. One of Dick's most admired middle-period novels. 131 pages.

title: Our Friends from Frolix 8
author: Philip K. Dick
card_summary: A 1970 novel set in a future where telepaths and precognitives form an oppressive ruling class. An ordinary man joins forces with a returning space explorer who brings an alien entity capable of destroying the elites' psychic powers. Mariner Books/Houghton Mifflin Harcourt edition in AZW3 format.

title: Radio Free Albemuth
author: Philip K. Dick
card_summary: Dick's original, more autobiographical version of VALIS, written in 1976 but published posthumously in 1985. The novel features a character named Philip K. Dick alongside his friend Nicholas Brady, who receives transmissions from an alien satellite called VALIS. The most directly autobiographical of Dick's novels. HMH Books 2020 EPUB edition.

title: Solar Lottery
author: Philip K. Dick
card_summary: Dick's first published novel (1955), set in a future where the leader of the solar system is chosen by a random lottery. When a new Quizmaster is selected, the previous regime dispatches an assassin android. A fast-paced debut that introduces many of Dick's enduring themes. Houghton Mifflin Harcourt/Mariner Books edition in AZW3 format.

title: The Cosmic Puppets
author: Philip K. Dick
card_summary: An early Dick novel (1957) in which a man returns to his hometown to find it completely transformed, with no one remembering the town he knew. Influenced by Zoroastrian mythology, the novel reveals a cosmic struggle between forces of light and darkness reshaping reality itself. Houghton Mifflin Harcourt EPUB edition.

title: The Crack in Space (Knopf Doubleday Edition)
author: Philip K. Dick
card_summary: A 1966 novel about an overpopulated Earth where a repairman discovers a crack in a Jiffi-scuttler appliance that opens a portal to a parallel Earth. Political machinations follow as factions compete to exploit the new world. Knopf Doubleday 2007 EPUB edition.

title: The Game-Players of Titan (Vintage Edition)
author: Philip K. Dick
card_summary: A 1963 novel set on an Earth depopulated by alien war, where survivors play a Monopoly-like game called Bluff with real property stakes, overseen by telepathic alien Vugs from Titan. This Vintage 2001 reprint edition runs 212 pages. One of Dick's mid-career explorations of game theory, deception, and alien manipulation.

title: The Ganymede Takeover
author: Philip K. Dick and Ray Nelson
card_summary: A collaborative novel by Dick and his friend Ray Nelson (1967), set on an Earth conquered by worm-like aliens from Ganymede. Human resistance forces use psychedelic drugs, music, and guerrilla tactics against the occupiers. The only novel Dick co-wrote with Nelson, his Berkeley friend and fellow SF writer. Ace Books edition, 164 pages.

title: The Man Who Japed (Ace Double D-193)
author: Philip K. Dick
card_summary: The original 1956 Ace Double edition of Dick's satirical novel about a future moralistic society where Allen Purcell, a propaganda agency director, discovers he has been unconsciously vandalizing the statue of the regime's founder. An early exploration of repression and subversion.

title: The Man in the High Castle
author: Philip K. Dick
card_summary: Dick's Hugo Award-winning alternate history novel (1962) set in a world where the Axis powers won World War II. America is partitioned between Nazi Germany and Imperial Japan, and the I Ching guides both characters and author. Vintage/Mariner Books 2016 EPUB edition of Dick's most celebrated work.

title: The Owl in Daylight
author: Tessa B. Dick
card_summary: Related to 'The Owl in Daylight,' the title of Philip K. Dick's unfinished final novel — a project he was working on at the time of his death in 1982. PKD described the novel as involving Beethoven and themes of music and alien perception. This document is likely Tessa B. Dick's published novel of the same name, written as a continuation or interpretation of PKD's concept. 110 pages.

title: The Penultimate Truth
author: Philip K. Dick
card_summary: A 1964 novel in which humanity lives in underground ant tanks, believing a nuclear war rages on the surface. In reality the war ended years ago, and the surface is a paradise controlled by an elite class using propaganda to keep the masses below. A prescient exploration of media manipulation and manufactured consent. 151 pages.

title: The Simulacra
author: Philip K. Dick
card_summary: A 1964 novel set in a future United States where the president is a simulacrum (android) and real power is held by a secret elite. Dick weaves together multiple storylines involving time travel, psychokinesis, and Martian emigration into a complex political satire about the artificiality of power. 222 pages.

title: The Unteleported Man (aka Lies, Inc.)
author: Philip K. Dick
card_summary: The 1983 Berkley edition of Dick's novel about Rachmael ben Applebaum, who suspects that teleportation to the colony planet Whale's Mouth is a one-way trap. Originally a 1966 novella expanded into a novel with hallucinatory additional material. Later retitled Lies, Inc. 153 pages.

title: The VALIS Trilogy
author: Philip K. Dick
card_summary: An omnibus edition collecting Philip K. Dick's three final novels: VALIS (1981), The Divine Invasion (1982), and The Transmigration of Timothy Archer (1982), published together by First Mariner Books in 2011. The trilogy represents Dick's most sustained engagement with theology, gnosticism, and the nature of divine reality, drawing heavily on his mystical experiences of 1974 and his Exegesis writings. Published by Houghton Mifflin Harcourt with a bibliography and author biography. Considered among the most philosophically ambitious works in Dick's canon.

title: The VALIS Trilogy
author: Philip K. Dick
card_summary: An omnibus edition collecting Dick's final three novels: VALIS (1981), The Divine Invasion (1981), and The Transmigration of Timothy Archer (1982). These works represent Dick's most autobiographical and theologically ambitious fiction, directly drawing on his 2-3-74 mystical experiences. Houghton Mifflin Harcourt edition in AZW3 format.

title: The Zap Gun
author: Philip K. Dick
card_summary: A 1967 satirical novel about weapons designers on both sides of the Cold War who channel designs through mediumistic trances, producing weapons that are secretly converted into consumer products. When a real alien threat appears, the sham weapons are useless. A dark comedy about the military-industrial complex. 209 pages.

title: Time Out of Joint
author: Philip K. Dick
card_summary: A 1959 novel in which Ragle Gumm, a man who wins newspaper contests for a living, gradually discovers that his entire small-town 1950s existence is a constructed illusion. One of Dick's earliest explorations of simulated reality, anticipating The Truman Show by decades. 155 pages.

title: Ubik
author: Philip K. Dick
card_summary: Philip K. Dick's novel Ubik, an e-text version (eVersion 1.1). The novel opens on the night of June 5, 1992, when the top telepath in the Sol System disappears from the offices of Runciter Associates in New York City, triggering a crisis for Glen Runciter's organization. Each chapter is prefaced by an advertising slogan for the enigmatic product 'Ubik'. The novel is a foundational work of Dick's career, exploring themes of corporate precognition, half-life states, temporal regression, and the unreliability of perceived reality. Widely regarded as one of Dick's finest achievements in science fiction.

title: Ubik
author: Philip K. Dick
card_summary: Widely regarded as one of Dick's masterpieces, Ubik (1969) follows Joe Chip and a team of anti-psychics whose reality begins deteriorating after a catastrophic explosion. Objects regress to earlier forms, and a mysterious product called Ubik may be the only defense against entropic decay. Houghton Mifflin Harcourt 2012 EPUB edition.

title: Voices from the Street
author: Philip K. Dick
card_summary: A mainstream novel written in the early 1950s and published posthumously in 2007. Set in a California electronics store, it follows a young salesman's disillusionment and existential crisis. Draws heavily on Dick's own experience working at Art Music in Berkeley. One of the last Dick novels to reach print. Ace Books EPUB edition.

title: A Scanner Darkly (Screenplay, First Draft)
author: Charlie Kaufman
card_summary: First draft screenplay dated December 20, 1997, written by Charlie Kaufman and adapted from Philip K. Dick's novel A Scanner Darkly. The script opens in Orange County, California in the year 1994 and follows Bob Arctor, depicting the same hallucinatory drug underworld as the source novel. Kaufman's adaptation precedes the 2006 Richard Linklater animated film and represents an important piece of the film adaptation history of Dick's fiction.

title: Best of If Magazine, Volume 1
author: Various (including Philip K. Dick)
card_summary: This is 'Best of If Magazine, Volume 1,' a 2021 anthology compiled by Christopher Broschell collecting classic stories from If magazine. The table of contents includes PKD stories such as Exhibit Piece alongside many other authors. If magazine was a prominent science fiction digest that published Dick and many other SF writers. This is a multi-author reprint anthology rather than a dedicated Philip K. Dick collection.

title: MR34443 (Unidentified Scanned Document)
author: Unknown
card_summary: A 160-page work identified by the reference code MR34443, possibly an archival catalog or accession number from a library, university archive, or manuscript repository. The substantial length indicates a book-length work, manuscript, or compiled document related to Philip K. Dick or his circle.

title: Philip K. Dick Papers – Special Collections Finding Aid
author: Unknown
card_summary: A brief informational document about the Philip K. Dick Papers held in Special Collections at what appears to be California State University, Fullerton (referenced via library.fullerton.edu). The text describes Special Collections as housing the library's rarest and most valuable original materials, and specifically notes holdings of original manuscripts by Philip K. Dick alongside materials related to Ray Bradbury, Frank Herbert, and the original Star Trek series. Also references the Boswell Collection on the History of Cartography and the Frederick and Patricia Shroyer Science Fiction collection. Directs readers to finding aids on the Online Archives of California.

title: Philip K. Dick is Dead, Alas
author: Michael Bishop
card_summary: A satirical alternate history novel by Michael Bishop set in a world where America won the Vietnam War and Richard Nixon was reelected four times. Philip K. Dick is remembered for realist novels while his SF circulates illegally in samizdat form. When a bearded amnesiac appears in a Georgia therapist's office, the boundaries between Dick's fiction and reality blur in a tribute to Dick's own literary preoccupations.

title: Prompt Rewriting Specialist
author: Unknown
card_summary: A document titled 'Prompt Rewriting Specialist.' Appears to be a reference sheet or instructional document related to AI prompt engineering or copywriting techniques. Does not appear to be directly related to Philip K. Dick or his work; may represent a misfiled item.

title: Research Note: Eric Foss Hour 25 Collection at UC Riverside (MS 054)
author: Unknown (archival research document)
card_summary: A research document providing contact and location information for the Eric Foss collection related to the Hour 25 science fiction radio program. The collection was donated to UC Riverside Special Collections in 2011 and is cataloged as MS 054 within the Eaton Collection of Science Fiction and Fantasy at Tomas Rivera Library. The document notes the collection is unprocessed and directs researchers to contact Special Collections directly. Useful as a reference guide for scholars investigating Hour 25 and its PKD-related broadcasts.

title: The Android and the Human (Manuscript) - Ken Lopez Bookseller Catalog
author: Philip K. Dick
card_summary: Philip K. Dick's original manuscript for 'The Android and the Human,' a speech delivered at the Vancouver SF Convention at the University of British Columbia in March 1972. The manuscript comprises 42 ribbon-copy typewritten pages with handwritten corrections and emendations by Dick. The speech was subsequently published in SF Commentary in December 1972 and reprinted in Vector in March-April 1973.

title: The Anecdote, Volume 6 (2016) - CSUF English Department Newsletter
author: California State University, Fullerton, Department of English, Comparative Literature, and Linguistics
card_summary: Volume 6 (2016) of The Anecdote, the newsletter of the Department of English, Comparative Literature, and Linguistics at California State University, Fullerton. The issue includes a letter from Department Chair Lana L. Dalley, articles on 'The Game Players and the Titans' by Emily Robles, 'In Service of Learning' by Chenglin Lee, and other departmental content. The newsletter's relevance to Philip K. Dick likely relates to CSUF's PKD archive and Dick's connection to the university's Special Collections. Encoding artifacts are present in portions of the text.

title: The Name of a Dog, or Natural Rights — Emmanuel Levinas
author: Emmanuel Levinas
card_summary: 'The Name of a Dog, or Natural Rights' by French philosopher Emmanuel Levinas. This celebrated essay reflects on a dog named Bobby encountered during Levinas's wartime internment and meditates on recognition, humanity, and ethical responsibility. Its presence in this collection likely reflects Dick's philosophical reading interests, as themes of personhood, consciousness, and what constitutes a 'human' resonate strongly with Dick's own fiction and Exegesis. Four pages.

title: The Religious Experience of Philip K. Dick (R. Crumb)
author: R. Crumb
card_summary: R. Crumb's celebrated comic strip adaptation 'The Religious Experience of Philip K. Dick,' originally published in Weirdo magazine in 1986. The strip depicts Dick's pivotal 2-3-74 mystical experiences in Crumb's distinctive underground comics style, dramatizing the visions of pink light, VALIS, and the anamnesis event that would dominate the last decade of Dick's life and writing. A significant piece of cultural reception of Dick's biography, frequently reproduced in studies of Dick's mystical experiences.

title: Upon the Dull Earth (Vocabulary Quiz / Study Guide)
author: Unknown (educator)
card_summary: A 5-page educational document containing a vocabulary quiz based on Philip K. Dick's short story "Upon the Dull Earth." The quiz presents passages from the story followed by multiple-choice vocabulary questions testing comprehension of words like "frantic," drawn from the story's atmospheric descriptions of Silvia and Rick encountering mysterious beings with distant flapping sounds like vast leaves rustling in a winter storm.

title: Cosmogony and Cosmology
author: Philip K. Dick
card_summary: A 1978 philosophical essay by Philip K. Dick in which he develops his concept of reality as a projected framework generated by an artifact he calls Zebra, a computerlike teaching machine. Dick argues that this artifact projects our world as a mirror for its maker (the Urgrund, a term borrowed from Jakob Boehme, 1616) to achieve self-awareness and self-knowledge. The essay is a key document in understanding Dick's late metaphysical and theological thinking, closely related to his ongoing Exegesis project.

title: How to Build a Universe That Doesn't Fall Apart Two Days Later
author: Philip K. Dick
card_summary: A celebrated 1978 essay and speech by Philip K. Dick in which he explores questions of reality, authenticity, and what it means for things and people to be real versus fake. Originally delivered as a speech, Dick weaves together personal anecdotes, philosophical reflection, and science-fictional speculation. He discusses Disneyland, fascism, and the nature of the universe with characteristic wit and depth. A key primary text for understanding his philosophical worldview.

title: How to Build a Universe That Doesn't Fall Apart Two Days Later
author: Philip K. Dick
card_summary: Philip K. Dick's celebrated 1978 essay "How to Build a Universe That Doesn't Fall Apart Two Days Later," one of his most widely read non-fiction works. Dick opens with greetings from Disneyland and an anecdote about being interviewed for Paris TV. The essay develops his philosophical preoccupations with the nature of reality, authenticity, and what it means to be human, delivered in Dick's characteristic blend of humor and metaphysical seriousness. Originally prepared as a speech, it became a landmark statement of his worldview.

title: In Pursuit of VALIS: Selections from the Exegesis
author: Philip K. Dick (edited by Lawrence Sutin)
card_summary: An Underwood-Miller anthology published in 1991 collecting selections from Philip K. Dick's vast private philosophical journal known as the Exegesis. Edited by Lawrence Sutin, with a preface by Sutin, an afterword by Terence McKenna, and an introduction by Jay Kinney. Also includes a PKD chronology by Paul Williams. The Exegesis documents Dick's intense mystical experiences beginning in February 1974 and his ongoing attempts to understand their theological and philosophical significance.

title: In Pursuit of VALIS: Selections from the Exegesis (OceanofPDF edition)
author: Philip K. Dick (edited by Lawrence Sutin)
card_summary: The 1991 Underwood-Miller anthology collecting selections from Philip K. Dick's private philosophical journal, the Exegesis. Edited by Lawrence Sutin, with a preface by Sutin, an afterword by Terence McKenna, and an introduction by Jay Kinney. Also includes a PKD chronology by Paul Williams.

title: PKD Exegesis Pages 1-6
author: Philip K. Dick
card_summary: Handwritten pages 1 through 6 from Philip K. Dick's Exegesis, the sprawling private philosophical and theological journal he maintained from 1974 until his death in 1982. This document is part of the broader Exegesis archive.

title: PKD Exegesis: August 1978, Folder 18, Pages 1-5
author: Philip K. Dick
card_summary: Pages 1 through 5 of Folder 18 of Philip K. Dick's Exegesis, dated August 1978. The Exegesis is Dick's monumental private journal of theological, philosophical, and metaphysical speculation, written primarily in response to his 2-3-74 visionary experiences. Folder 18 falls within the mid-period of the Exegesis composition.

title: PKD Exegesis: August 1978, Folder 18, Pages 6-10
author: Philip K. Dick
card_summary: Pages 6 through 10 of Folder 18 of Philip K. Dick's Exegesis, dated August 1978. Continuing from the preceding pages 1-5, this represents the second half of the same folder from Dick's vast private theological and philosophical journal.

title: Philip K. Dick Reviews Herman Wouk's The Caine Mutiny (Time, October 3, 1955)
author: Philip K. Dick
card_summary: Philip K. Dick's review of Herman Wouk's novel 'The Caine Mutiny', published in Time magazine on October 3, 1955. This represents a rare early piece of PKD non-fiction criticism, written during his most prolific short-story period. Dick's engagement with a major mainstream literary work like Wouk's Pulitzer Prize-winning novel is of biographical and critical interest.

title: Schizophrenia and the Book of Changes
author: Philip K. Dick
card_summary: Philip K. Dick's essay 'Schizophrenia and the Book of Changes,' a non-fiction philosophical piece in which Dick explores the relationship between schizophrenic experience and the I Ching (Book of Changes). The essay reflects Dick's lifelong engagement with Eastern philosophy, altered states of consciousness, and the nature of reality. One of several non-fiction essays Dick wrote examining the boundaries between mental illness, perception, and metaphysical insight.

title: The Earthshaker: Novel Outline
author: Philip K. Dick
card_summary: A chapter-by-chapter outline by Philip K. Dick for an unpublished novel titled The Earthshaker, also referencing characters named the Wanderer, the Miracle Child (Benjie), and the Earth Woman. The outline sketches approximately nine or more chapters organized into structural sections: 'Coming,' 'Being,' and a first 'Aecum or Ascent.' Key plot elements include a wanderer returning to his village, the miraculous child Benjie, an ambiguous mission involving something called 'the Earthshaker,' and a salamander-flames motif. The document appears to be a manuscript working draft.

title: The Exegesis of Philip K. Dick
author: Philip K. Dick (edited by Pamela Jackson and Jonathan Lethem)
card_summary: The edited and annotated publication of Philip K. Dick's vast private philosophical journal, compiled over the final years of his life as he attempted to understand a series of mystical experiences beginning in February 1974. Published by Houghton Mifflin Harcourt in 2011, the volume was edited by Pamela Jackson and Jonathan Lethem, with an introduction by both editors and an afterword by Richard Doyle. Organized in four parts, it includes a glossary, names index, works index, and extensive endnotes. Represents PKD's most sustained non-fiction philosophical writing.

title: The Exegesis of Philip K. Dick
author: Philip K. Dick (edited by Pamela Jackson and Jonathan Lethem)
card_summary: The published edition of Philip K. Dick's Exegesis, released in 2011 by Houghton Mifflin Harcourt, edited by Pamela Jackson and Jonathan Lethem with annotations by a team of scholars. The volume collects Dick's massive private journal of philosophical, theological, and metaphysical speculation written between 1974 and 1982 following his visionary experiences of February-March 1974 (2-3-74). Organized in four parts with an introduction by Lethem and Jackson and an afterword by Richard Doyle, the edition includes a glossary, names index, and works index. Copyright held by Laura Coelho, Christopher Dick, and Isa Hackett.

title: The Shifting Realities of Philip K. Dick: Selected Literary and Philosophical Writings
author: Philip K. Dick (edited by Lawrence Sutin)
card_summary: A curated anthology of Philip K. Dick's non-fiction writings edited by Lawrence Sutin and published as a First Vintage Books Edition in 1995. The collection assembles philosophical essays, journal entries, speeches, and plot scenarios in which Dick explores the nature of reality, sanity, and humanity. Widely praised as essential non-fiction Dick, the volume is described by the Boston Phoenix as unearthing 'some gems' for both casual and serious readers. Demonstrates Dick's parallel identity as a visionary philosopher alongside his career as a science fiction novelist.

title: Ubik: The Screenplay
author: Philip K. Dick
card_summary: Philip K. Dick's screenplay adaptation of his 1969 novel Ubik, written for a proposed but ultimately unproduced film. The volume includes a foreword, the full screenplay text across more than forty numbered scenes, and an afterword by Dick titled 'Ubik: An Afterword'. The screenplay demonstrates Dick's own engagement with the adaptation process and his vision for translating the novel's complex metaphysical themes to the screen. A significant primary document in understanding both Dick's craft and the history of attempts to film his work.

title: Ubik: The Screenplay
author: Philip K. Dick
card_summary: Dick's own screenplay adaptation of his novel Ubik, written before his death in 1982 and published posthumously in 1985. Dick reimagined his novel for film, making significant changes to the narrative. A fascinating document of Dick's engagement with Hollywood and his vision for how his fiction should be adapted. Houghton Mifflin Harcourt 2012 EPUB edition.

title: "I Am a Machine": Programming and Control in Dick's Exegesis (PKD Festival 2024)
author: Gabriel McKee
card_summary: A slide presentation delivered by Gabriel McKee at the Philip K. Dick Festival on June 14, 2024. The talk, titled 'I Am a Machine: Programming and Control in Dick's Exegesis,' examines themes of mechanical determinism and control within Dick's Exegesis. Slides reference the Zebrapedia Project transcription archive, Exegesis folders 82, 34, 22, and 90, a letter from PKD to Claudia Bush (July 30, 1974), the first German edition of VALIS (Moewig, 1984), and imagery from Athanasius Kircher's Ars Magna Lucis et Umbrae (1646).

title: Archaeologies of the Future: The Desire Called Utopia and Other Science Fictions
author: Fredric Jameson
card_summary: A major work of literary and cultural criticism by Fredric Jameson, published by Verso in 2005. At 431 pages, the book examines utopian thought and science fiction as expressions of political desire and historical imagination. Jameson is one of the most influential Marxist literary critics of the twentieth century, and his engagement with Dick's work (particularly in his earlier essay on Dick and postmodernism) helped establish Dick as a figure of serious theoretical interest.

title: Authors of the Impossible: The Paranormal and the Sacred
author: Jeffrey J. Kripal
card_summary: A study by Rice University religious studies professor Jeffrey Kripal arguing that paranormal phenomena deserve serious intellectual engagement. Drawing on cases from psychical research, UFO encounters, and science fiction, Kripal proposes that the paranormal represents a form of the sacred that challenges conventional categories of both science and religion.

title: Call for Papers: 2015 Eaton Science Fiction Conference — Designing the Past, Designing the Future
author: Eaton Science Fiction Conference Program Committee
card_summary: The official Call for Papers for the 2015 Eaton Science Fiction Conference, titled "Designing the Past, Designing the Future," held October 16-17, 2015 at the Riverside Marriott Hotel in Riverside, California. The CFP solicits paper and panel proposals examining the relationship between speculative genres (science fiction, fantasy, horror, utopian literature) and the concept of design. Topics include world-building aesthetics, contemporaneous design philosophies, engineering, advertising, and architecture as science-fictional discourses.

title: Comprehensive Audit of Philip K. Dick's Documented Public Appearances, Speeches, and Interviews
author: Unknown (compiled research document)
card_summary: A compiled audit of Philip K. Dick's documented public appearances, speeches, and interviews, drawing on philipidick.com archives, Frank C. Bertrand's research on missing or problem interviews, The Last Interview anthology, and fan scholarship databases. Key findings include that Dick's public-facing activity was concentrated between 1972 and 1982, with only three formal speeches documented: "The Android and the Human" (Vancouver, 1972), "If You Find This World Bad" (Metz, 1977), and "How to Build a Universe" (1978). His only international trip is also noted.

title: Deleuze's Dick
author: Unknown
card_summary: A 32-page scholarly essay examining the intersection of Gilles Deleuze's philosophy and Philip K. Dick's fiction. The paper explores how Deleuze's concepts of difference, repetition, virtuality, and the fold illuminate Dick's literary constructions of unstable realities and fragmented subjectivities. Represents the growing body of continental philosophical engagement with Dick's work.

title: Dick, Deception, and Dissociation: A Comment on 'The Two Faces of Philip K. Dick'
author: Gregg Rickman
card_summary: A response article from Science Fiction Studies (Vol. 18, No. 2, July 1991) by Gregg Rickman, Dick's biographer, commenting on Robert Philmus's article 'The Two Faces of Philip K. Dick.' Rickman brings his deep biographical knowledge to bear on questions of deception and dissociation in Dick's life and work. 5 pages.

title: Errata: L'oscuro scrutare di Philip K. Dick (Review)
author: Umberto Rossi
card_summary: A review/errata notice from Science Fiction Studies (Vol. 35, No. 1, March 2008) by Umberto Rossi regarding his Italian-language study of Dick, L'oscuro scrutare di Philip K. Dick (Philip K. Dick's Dark Scanning). Reflects the international dimension of Dick scholarship. 2 pages.

title: Foundation: The Review of Science Fiction, Issue 25 (June 1982)
author: David Pringle (editor); Ian Watson (Features Editor); John Clute (Reviews Editor)
card_summary: Issue 25 (June 1982) of Foundation: The Review of Science Fiction, edited by David Pringle. The issue features Thomas M. Disch on SF as a church, Phyllis Eisenstein on her sources, John Sladek on his midwestern childhood, W.M.S. Russell on folktales and SF, Gregory Feeley on Jack Dann, and Sue Jenkins comparing Star Trek and Blake's Seven. Reviews by Barbour, Clute, Greenland, Langford, Stableford, Wolfe and others cover works by Attanasio, Moorcock, Niven, Pohl, and more. Letters from Chauvin, Moorcock, and Stine are included.

title: Gnostic Awakenings: Science Fiction, Myth-Making, and the Religious Imagination
author: Daniel Atik
card_summary: A Wesleyan University honors thesis in Religion exploring the intersection of science fiction, myth-making, and the religious imagination. Examines how science fiction authors, including Philip K. Dick, engage with Gnostic themes and religious meaning-making through speculative storytelling.

title: Gnosticism and Dualism in the Early Fiction of Philip K. Dick
author: Lorenzo DiTommaso
card_summary: A scholarly article from Science Fiction Studies examining the Gnostic and dualistic themes in Philip K. Dick's early fiction. DiTommaso traces Dick's engagement with Gnostic cosmology and the persistent dualism between authentic and counterfeit reality that would become central to his mature work.

title: God and Human Beings in the Technologically-Mediated Living Condition: Philip K. Dick's Literary Theology in the VALIS Trilogy
author: Laurie Jui-hua Tseng
card_summary: An academic article exploring Philip K. Dick's literary theology in the VALIS trilogy, arguing that Dick portrays the technologically-mediated human condition as a simulacrum of God's divine matrix. Examines the concepts of God as Zebra, the distinction between divine and technological systems through love and living presence, and Dick's concealment of his Gnostic leanings.

title: Gregg Rickman and Others on Philip K. Dick (Review)
author: Istvan Csicsery-Ronay, Jr.
card_summary: Review essay by Istvan Csicsery-Ronay, Jr. in Science Fiction Studies (Vol. 22, No. 3, November 1995) reviewing Samuel J. Umland's "Philip K. Dick: Contemporary Critical Interpretations" alongside Gregg Rickman's contributions to Dick scholarship.

title: Le reel attrape par l'imaginaire: Philip Dick et la science-fonctionnalisation de la realite
author: Richard Saint-Gelais
card_summary: A French-language scholarly article from Etudes litteraires (Vol. 30, No. 1, April 2005) examining how Dick's fiction captures and transforms reality through the imaginary. Saint-Gelais explores the concept of 'science-functionalization' of reality in Dick's work. Part of the strong Francophone tradition of Dick scholarship. 15 pages.

title: Machines Making Gods: Philip K. Dick, Henri Bergson and Saint Paul
author: James Burton
card_summary: A scholarly article from Theory, Culture & Society (Vol. 25, No. 7-8, December 2008) connecting Dick's fiction to the philosophy of Henri Bergson and the theology of Saint Paul. Burton, who later wrote a full monograph on Dick and Bergson, examines the relationship between technology, divinity, and creative evolution. 23 pages.

title: Managers as Androids: Reading Moral Agency in Philip Dick
author: N. Srinivas
card_summary: A scholarly article from Organization (Vol. 6, No. 4, November 1999) reading Dick's android fiction through the lens of organizational theory and moral agency. Srinivas uses Dick's exploration of human-android boundaries to interrogate corporate management and the dehumanization of workplace relations. 16 pages.

title: Mutants & Mystics: Science Fiction, Superhero Comics, and the Paranormal
author: Jeffrey J. Kripal
card_summary: Jeffrey Kripal examines the deep connections between paranormal experience and the creation of science fiction and superhero comics. Through case studies of authors and artists including Philip K. Dick, Kripal argues that popular culture genres encode genuine encounters with the extraordinary, functioning as modern forms of mythological and mystical expression.

title: Objective Reduction of the Wave Function Demonstrated on Superconducting Quantum Computer
author: James Tagg, William Reid (Valis Corporation)
card_summary: A scientific paper by James Tagg and William Reid of Valis Corporation (Encinitas, California), dated April 2, 2025, describing a quantum computing experiment testing Orchestrated Objective Reduction (Orch-OR), the theory of consciousness proposed by Roger Penrose and Stuart Hameroff. The experiment uses superconducting transmon qubits to demonstrate wavefunction collapse consistent with Orch-OR. The organization name 'Valis Corporation' directly references PKD's novel VALIS, linking this modern quantum consciousness research to Dick's philosophical preoccupations with the nature of reality and mind. 8 pages.

title: On Our Philip K. Dick Collection (with Response)
author: Gregg Rickman; I. C. R.
card_summary: Exchange between Gregg Rickman and I. C. R. (Istvan Csicsery-Ronay) in Science Fiction Studies (Vol. 20, No. 1, March 1993) about the journal's Philip K. Dick special collection, discussing approaches to Dick scholarship and the state of Dick studies.

title: Ontological Instability and the Internal Author in the Fiction of Philip K. Dick
author: Ciarán Kavanagh
card_summary: A Master of Arts thesis from the National University of Ireland, Cork, examining ontological instability and the concept of the internal author in Philip K. Dick's fiction. Explores how Dick's narratives destabilize the reader's sense of reality and embed authorial self-reflection within the fictional world.

title: Ontology and Ethics in the Writings of Philip K. Dick (PhD Thesis)
author: Andrew Mark Butler
card_summary: A doctoral thesis submitted to the University of Hull in October 1995 by Andrew Mark Butler (B.A. Hons), examining ontology and ethics across Philip K. Dick's body of work. The thesis is structured in at least two chapters: the first addresses realism and the fantastic in Dick's fiction, including literary realism, author-reader relations, and the 'New Wave'; the second explores ontology and its discontents, engaging with postmodernism, Fredric Jameson, and Dick's narrative viewpoint techniques. Analyzes works including In Milton Lumky Territory. A substantial work of 313 pages representing serious academic engagement with Dick's literary and philosophical output.

title: PKD Collected Stories: Corrections Document
author: Unknown
card_summary: Corrections, errata, or editorial notes relating to a collected edition of Philip K. Dick's short stories. Likely a reader-submitted or editorially prepared list of textual corrections for one of the published volumes of Dick's collected fiction.

title: Philip K. Dick
author: Andrew M. Butler
card_summary: A critical introduction to Philip K. Dick's life and work by scholar Andrew M. Butler. The text surveys Dick's major themes including fake realities, questions of identity, the nature of consciousness, and his exploration of counter-cultural ideas. Butler positions Dick as a uniquely brilliant SF writer who probed the landscapes of the mind decades ahead of his time. Do Androids Dream of Electric Sheep?, the basis for Blade Runner, is highlighted as his most widely known work. Aimed at readers new to Dick's fiction.

title: Philip K. Dick (Pocket Essentials, Revised and Updated)
author: Andrew M. Butler
card_summary: A revised and updated edition of Andrew M. Butler's critical guide to Philip K. Dick, published by Pocket Essentials (Oldcastle Books) in 2007. At 161 pages, it provides an accessible introduction to Dick's major works and themes. Part of the Pocket Essentials series alongside volumes on Cyberpunk, Terry Pratchett, Film Studies, and Postmodernism. Butler, who also wrote a PhD thesis on Dick at the University of Hull, is one of the most prominent Dick scholars.

title: Philip K. Dick Estate Legal Research: IP Litigation, Copyright Renewal, and Commercialization Strategies (1982-2026)
author: Unknown (legal research document)
card_summary: A detailed legal research document examining the intellectual property management, copyright renewal discrepancies, and commercialization strategies of the Philip K. Dick Testamentary Trust and Electric Shepherd Productions from 1982 to 2026. The document analyzes post-mortem estate administration challenges for prolific authors, using Dick's fragmented corpus of novels, novellas, and short stories as a central case study. Topics include multi-generational IP protection, entertainment industry licensing, and legal complexities arising from Dick's highly fragmented literary estate. 20 pages.

title: Philip K. Dick Extant Fiction Chronology
author: Frank Hollander
card_summary: A fan-compiled chronology of Philip K. Dick's extant completed fiction, researched and sequenced by Frank Hollander between July and November 2012. The document establishes a coherent reading order for Dick's fiction, using the Scott Meredith Literary Agency (SMLA) submission date as its primary organizing principle. It acknowledges similarity to Lord RC's chronology in Pink Beam (2006) while focusing specifically on helping readers navigate the canon. Three major breaks in the sequence correspond to Dick's geographic relocations and personal life transitions.

title: Philip K. Dick Manuscript Collection Finding Aid (CSUF, SC.6, 2016)
author: CSU Fullerton University Archives & Special Collections
card_summary: The 2016 finding aid for the Philip K. Dick Manuscript Collection (Collection SC.6) held at California State University, Fullerton University Archives and Special Collections. The collection spans bulk dates 1959 to 1985 and comprises 14 document boxes. The finding aid describes access conditions, publication rights (property rights reside with CSUF), and provides researchers with an authoritative guide to the archival holdings of Dick's manuscripts. An essential reference tool for PKD scholars.

title: Philip K. Dick Public Appearances Audit
author: Unknown
card_summary: A research document auditing the full scope of Philip K. Dick's documented public appearances across his life and career. Drawing on biographer Paul Williams's characterization of Dick as a 'reluctant convention-goer' and the introduction to The Last Interview and Other Conversations, the document establishes that Dick's public presence was concentrated primarily between 1972 and 1982, with very little documented activity during his most prolific publishing period of the 1960s. The audit begins with Dick's first major public lecture at the University of British Columbia in February 1972.

title: Philip K. Dick Public Appearances Timeline (1955-1982)
author: Unknown
card_summary: A chronological timeline consolidating every documented instance of Philip K. Dick speaking publicly, appearing at conventions, giving interviews (in-person, radio, or television), or making other public-facing appearances across his lifetime (1928-1982). Each entry records the date, event description, type of appearance, location, and primary source documentation. The earliest documented appearance is a January 10, 1955 Oakland Tribune profile and interview, published when Dick was 26. The document confirms that the vast majority of documented appearances are concentrated in the final decade of Dick's life.

title: Philip K. Dick and the Umbrella of Light
author: Angus Taylor
card_summary: An early critical monograph on Philip K. Dick by Angus Taylor, originally published in 1975 by T-K Graphics, Baltimore, with illustrations by Cora Lee Healy. Parts of the work appeared earlier in the British journal Foundation (No. 4, July 1973) under the title 'Can God Fly? Can He Hold Out His Arms and Fly?' The work is structured in three parts examining monsters from outer and inner space, the willing extension of disbelief, and the concept of the 'umbrella of light.' This edition is a 2025 reformatted reissue with minor corrections. A significant early work of PKD literary criticism.

title: Philip K. Dick's Opus: Artifice as Refuge and World View (1975)
author: Darko Suvin
card_summary: Chapter 9 of a scholarly volume, originally published 1975, presenting a critical analysis of Philip K. Dick's literary opus under the title 'Philip K. Dick's Opus: Artifice as Refuge and World View'. The author divides Dick's writing into three main periods: 1952-62 (apprenticeship, culminating in The Man in the High Castle), 1962-65 (a 'high plateau' including Martian Time-Slip and Dr. Bloodmoney), and 1966-74. The chapter provides detailed close readings of individual novels and situates Dick within broader SF literary history. A foundational piece of early PKD academic scholarship. 60 pages.

title: Philip K. Dick: Addresses and Residences Chronology
author: Unknown
card_summary: A reference document cataloguing every known address and residence of Philip K. Dick from his birth in Chicago in 1928 through his adult life in California. Each entry includes the years of residence, the full street address, and notes on significant life events or works associated with that location. The chronology covers Dick's birth at 7812 Emerald Avenue, Chicago; early childhood in Greeley, Colorado; years in Washington D.C.; relocation to Berkeley; and his adult residences including 2328 Telegraph Avenue and 1126 Francisco Street in Berkeley, where he lived with his wife Kleo.

title: Philip K. Dick: Authenticity and Insincerity
author: John Huntington
card_summary: A scholarly article from the special PKD issue of Science Fiction Studies (Vol. 15, No. 2, July 1988) examining the tension between authenticity and insincerity in Dick's fiction. Huntington explores how Dick's characters navigate genuine and counterfeit experience. 10 pages.

title: Philip K. Dick: Contemporary Critical Interpretations
author: Samuel J. Umland (ed.)
card_summary: An edited collection of critical essays on Philip K. Dick published by Greenwood Press, a major academic publisher. At 120 pages, the volume brings together contemporary scholarly interpretations of Dick's work. Samuel J. Umland is a scholar who has contributed significantly to the academic study of Dick's fiction.

title: Philip K. Dick: Essays of the Here and Now
author: David Sandner
card_summary: A volume of critical essays on Philip K. Dick edited or authored by David Sandner, published as part of McFarland's Critical Explorations in Science Fiction and Fantasy series edited by Donald E. Palumbo and C.W. Sullivan III. The series includes numerous academic studies of SF authors and themes. This collection focuses on Dick's engagement with themes of the present, reality, and everyday life. It sits within a rich tradition of McFarland scholarship on science fiction and fantasy literature.

title: Philip K. Dick: Essays of the Here and Now
author: David Sandner, Donald E. Palumbo, and C.W. Sullivan III (eds.)
card_summary: An edited collection of critical essays on Philip K. Dick, published by McFarland as part of the Critical Explorations in Science Fiction and Fantasy series (edited by Donald E. Palumbo and C.W. Sullivan III). At 227 pages, the volume brings together multiple scholars examining Dick's continuing relevance to contemporary culture and thought. The series includes dozens of volumes on major SF and fantasy topics and authors.

title: Philip K. Dick: Exhilaration and Terror of the Postmodern
author: Christopher Palmer
card_summary: An academic study published by Liverpool University Press as part of the Liverpool Science Fiction Texts and Studies series (edited by David Seed). At 272 pages, Christopher Palmer examines Dick's work through the lens of postmodern theory, exploring how his fiction generates both exhilaration and terror in its engagement with the dissolution of stable reality. The series includes works by major SF scholars including Robert Crossley on Stapledon and David Seed on anticipation in SF.

title: Pink Light and Iron: Epistemo-Critical Writing in Walter Benjamin and Philip K. Dick
author: John McLoughlin
card_summary: A Cardiff University doctoral thesis comparing the labyrinthine, non-linear approaches to philosophical representation in Walter Benjamin's Arcades Project and The Exegesis of Philip K. Dick. Argues that both works embody a pluralistic notion of truth that can only be glimpsed indirectly, exploring what McLoughlin calls epistemo-critical writing.

title: Precognitive Scientific Information in the Fiction of Philip K. Dick: Will the Real Ubik Please Stand Up?
author: William Sarill
card_summary: A 37-page academic paper by William Sarill of the New Genesis Institute, Arlington, MA, presented at the 33rd Annual Conference of the Society for Scientific Exploration on June 6, 2014. The paper examines whether PKD's fiction contains precognitive scientific information — ideas that anticipated later scientific discoveries — using Ubik as a primary case study. It surveys PKD's career of 44 novels and 121 stories, his autodidactic engagement with metaphysics and altered states of consciousness, and his central thematic preoccupations with the nature of reality and humanity.

title: Reality as Ideological Construct: A Reading of Five Novels by Philip K. Dick
author: Peter Fitting
card_summary: A Marxist-informed scholarly article from Science Fiction Studies (Vol. 10, No. 2, July 1983) reading five Dick novels through the lens of ideology and constructed reality. Fitting examines how Dick's fictional realities expose the ideological construction of consensus reality. 19 pages.

title: Review: Mutants and Mystics by Jeffrey J. Kripal (Journal of Religion)
author: William Elison
card_summary: A scholarly book review from The Journal of Religion evaluating Jeffrey J. Kripal's Mutants and Mystics, which examines the connections between science fiction, superhero comics, and paranormal experience. The review assesses Kripal's argument that popular culture genres serve as vehicles for genuine mystical and paranormal encounters.

title: Review: Search for Philip K. Dick, 1928-1982 by Anne Dick
author: Michael J. Tolley
card_summary: A book review from Utopian Studies (Vol. 7, No. 2, 1996) by Michael J. Tolley reviewing Anne Dick's memoir and biography Search for Philip K. Dick, 1928-1982. Anne Dick was Philip's third wife, and her account provides intimate biographical details from their marriage during the early 1960s. 3 pages.

title: Review: TechGnosis by Erik Davis (Fortnight)
author: Brendan Duffin
card_summary: A brief book review of Erik Davis's TechGnosis published in the Irish magazine Fortnight in November 1999. Reviews Davis's exploration of the intersection between mysticism, myth, and information technology from the printing press to the internet age.

title: Review: TechGnosis by Erik Davis (Leonardo)
author: Kevin Murray
card_summary: A review of Erik Davis's TechGnosis published in Leonardo, the MIT Press journal of art, science, and technology. Evaluates Davis's argument about the persistent entanglement of mystical and magical thinking with the development of information technologies.

title: Rosicrucian PKD Sources
author: Unknown (compiler)
card_summary: A 32-page research document compiling Rosicrucian and esoteric source materials relevant to Philip K. Dick's metaphysical thinking. Draws from the VALIS appendix (Tractates Cryptica Scriptura), referencing Apollonius of Tyana, Hermes Trismegistos, Paul of Tarsus, Simon Magus, Asklepios, Paracelsus, Boehme, and Bruno. Explores Dick's engagement with holographic universe theory, the concept of backward time movement, and the hermetic tradition.

title: Science Fiction in the Real World
author: Norman Spinrad
card_summary: A 1990 critical volume by science fiction author and critic Norman Spinrad, part of the Southern Illinois University Press 'Alternatives' series edited by Eric S. Rabkin, Martin H. Greenberg, and Joseph D. Olander. The book collects Spinrad's essays on science fiction as a literary genre and cultural phenomenon. It likely includes discussion of Philip K. Dick given Spinrad's close acquaintance with Dick and his critical engagement with Dick's work throughout his career.

title: Space Opera: The Aesthetics of Personhood in the Works and Worlds of Philip K. Dick
author: Gabriel Francis Mamola
card_summary: A doctoral dissertation from the University of Texas at Arlington examining the aesthetics of personhood in the works and worlds of Philip K. Dick. Analyzes how Dick's fiction constructs, interrogates, and deconstructs the concept of personhood through the lens of science fiction's formal and thematic possibilities.

title: TechGnosis: Myth, Magic & Mysticism in the Age of Information
author: Erik Davis
card_summary: A wide-ranging cultural study exploring the mystical and mythological impulses underlying the development of information technology, from the printing press through radio, television, and the internet. Davis traces how Gnostic, hermetic, and magical traditions have persistently shaped humanity's relationship with technology, with significant attention to science fiction authors including Philip K. Dick.

title: The Apocalyptic Vision of Philip K. Dick
author: Steven Best and Douglas Kellner
card_summary: An academic essay by Steven Best (University of Texas at El Paso) and Douglas Kellner (UCLA) titled 'The Apocalyptic Vision of Philip K. Dick'. The paper situates Dick's fiction within the context of postindustrial capitalism, technological revolution, and the global restructuring of the economy. Drawing on critical theory and cultural studies frameworks, Best and Kellner analyze how Dick's work anticipates and responds to what they term the 'great transformation' of the late twentieth century — the shift to infotainment, biotech, and information capitalism. 15 pages.

title: The Encounter of Taoism and Fascism in The Man in the High Castle
author: Patricia Warrick
card_summary: A foundational scholarly article from Science Fiction Studies (Vol. 7, No. 2, July 1980) by Patricia Warrick examining the philosophical tension between Taoism and fascism in Dick's The Man in the High Castle. Warrick was one of the earliest and most important academic Dick scholars. 18 pages.

title: The French Dick: Villiers de l'Isle-Adam, Philip K. Dick, and the Android
author: Ritch Calvin
card_summary: A scholarly article from Extrapolation (Vol. 48, No. 2, January 2007) examining the literary connection between French symbolist writer Villiers de l'Isle-Adam and Philip K. Dick through the figure of the android. Calvin traces how both authors explored artificial beings as vehicles for questioning authenticity and humanity. 24 pages.

title: The Holy Family from Outer Space: Reconsidering Philip K. Dick's The Divine Invasion
author: Umberto Rossi
card_summary: A scholarly article from Extrapolation (Vol. 52, No. 2, January 2011) in which Umberto Rossi reconsiders Dick's novel The Divine Invasion through the concept of the holy family. Rossi, a prominent Italian Dick scholar, examines the theological and familial structures in one of Dick's most overtly religious late novels. 21 pages.

title: The I Ching and Philip K. Dick's The Man in the High Castle
author: Paul Mountfort
card_summary: A scholarly article from Science Fiction Studies (Vol. 43, No. 2, 2016) examining Dick's use of the I Ching in writing and structuring The Man in the High Castle. Mountfort explores how the ancient Chinese oracle functioned as both narrative device and compositional method. 24 pages.

title: The Nature of Dick's Fantasies (Review)
author: Gregg Rickman
card_summary: Review essay by Gregg Rickman in Science Fiction Studies (Vol. 19, No. 1, March 1992). Reviews Lawrence Sutin's "In Pursuit of Valis: Selections from the Exegesis" and "The Selected Letters of Philip K. Dick." Discusses the nature of Dick's visionary and fantastical experiences as documented in the Exegesis and correspondence.

title: The Novels of Philip K. Dick
author: Kim Stanley Robinson
card_summary: Kim Stanley Robinson's doctoral dissertation on Philip K. Dick's novels, published by UMI Research Press as No. 9 in the Studies in Speculative Fiction series (edited by Robert Scholes of Brown University). At 153 pages, this is one of the earliest and most important academic studies of Dick's fiction, written by Robinson before he became one of the most celebrated science fiction authors of his generation (Mars trilogy, The Ministry for the Future).

title: The Philosophy of Science Fiction: Henri Bergson and the Fabulations of Philip K. Dick
author: James Burton
card_summary: An academic study by James Burton published by Bloomsbury Academic that reads Philip K. Dick's fiction through the philosophy of Henri Bergson. At 244 pages, the book connects Dick's explorations of time, memory, perception, and reality to Bergson's philosophical framework. Part of a broader intellectual conversation that includes related Bloomsbury publications on Bergson and a biography of Dick by Emmanuel Carrere.

title: The Psychogeography of Philip K. Dick: A Comprehensive Audit of Residential, Professional, and Spiritual Landmarks
author: Unknown (researcher)
card_summary: A research report providing an exhaustive audit of Philip K. Dick's residential history, tracing the physical addresses he inhabited across his lifetime and analyzing their relationship to his fiction. The document maps Dick's movements from the Midwest to Berkeley, Marin County, and Southern California, arguing that these environments served as architectural templates for the 'conapts' and alternate worlds in his novels. The report verifies established biographical data, identifies omissions in standard biographical accounts, and synthesizes the relationship between place and Dick's prolific literary output. 15 pages.

title: The Transcendent Function (Chapter One)
author: Unknown
card_summary: Chapter One of a work titled 'The Transcendent Function,' presenting an introduction to the concept of the transcendent function as it relates to the development of the ego in Western consciousness. The chapter surveys the trajectory of Western civilization from ancient Greece through Christianity, the Renaissance, the Scientific Revolution, and into modernity, framing this history around the Cartesian cogito and rational individualism. The Jungian term 'transcendent function' and the philosophical approach suggest this may be a psychological or academic work. Its precise connection to Philip K. Dick studies is not determinable from the excerpt alone.

title: The Two Faces of Philip K. Dick
author: Robert M. Philmus
card_summary: A scholarly article from Science Fiction Studies (Vol. 18, No. 1, March 1991) examining duality and contradictions in Dick's literary persona and work. Philmus's article prompted a response from biographer Gregg Rickman in a subsequent issue. 14 pages.

title: The World According to Philip K. Dick
author: Alexander Dunst and Stefan Schlensag (editors)
card_summary: A 2015 scholarly essay collection edited by Alexander Dunst (University of Paderborn) and Stefan Schlensag (TU Dortmund University), published by Palgrave Macmillan. The volume gathers academic perspectives on Philip K. Dick's fiction, likely covering themes of reality, identity, technology, and Dick's cultural legacy. The document appears to be a preview or metadata page from the publisher's website, indicating this is a subscription-access academic title with over 3,800 recorded accesses.

title: Third Reality: On the Persistence of Philip K. Dick (Introduction)
author: Alexander Dunst
card_summary: An academic introductory essay by Alexander Dunst titled 'Third Reality: On the Persistence of Philip K. Dick.' Drawing on a 1972 letter in which PKD reflects on Southern California life and the nature of science fiction, Dunst explores Dick's philosophical concepts of idios kosmos and koinos kosmos (private versus shared reality) and their disruption by apparent hallucination. Dunst frames these concepts as constituting a 'third reality' between solid fact and pure illusion, arguing that this framework remains central to understanding Dick's enduring cultural and literary significance.

title: Towards a Theory of Paranoia: The Science Fiction of Philip K. Dick
author: Carl Freedman
card_summary: An influential scholarly article from Science Fiction Studies (Vol. 11, No. 1, March 1984) in which Carl Freedman develops a theoretical framework for understanding paranoia as a structural element in Dick's fiction. Freedman later became one of the leading theorists of science fiction. 11 pages.

title: Will the Real Ubik Please Stand Up? (EdgeScience #20)
author: William Sarill
card_summary: An article by William Sarill titled 'Will the Real Ubik Please Stand Up? Precognition of Scientific Information in the Fiction of Philip K. Dick,' published in EdgeScience magazine issue 20 (December 2014), a quarterly publication of the Society for Scientific Exploration. The article investigates instances of apparent precognition in Dick's fiction, examining how his stories anticipated real scientific developments. The issue also includes other unrelated articles on time and mystery snakes.

title: Worlds Built to Fall Apart: Versions of Philip K. Dick
author: David Lapoujade (trans. Erik Beranek)
card_summary: A philosophical study of Philip K. Dick by French philosopher David Lapoujade, translated from French by Erik Beranek and published by the University of Minnesota Press. At 206 pages, the book examines Dick's literary worlds as constructions designed to collapse, exploring how instability and disintegration function as creative and philosophical principles in his fiction. Part of a distinguished series featuring works by major continental philosophers including Baudrillard, Derrida, Deleuze, Guattari, and Didi-Huberman.

title: Electric Dreams
author: Philip K. Dick
card_summary: A collection of ten Philip K. Dick short stories published in conjunction with the Electric Dreams television anthology series. Each story is preceded by an introduction from the respective TV episode's screenwriter or producer, including Ronald D. Moore, Jack Thorne, David Farr, Dee Rees, and others. Stories included are: Exhibit Piece, The Commuter, The Impossible Planet, The Hanging Stranger, Sales Pitch, The Father-Thing, The Hood Maker, Foster You're Dead, Human Is, and Autofac.

title: Second Variety and Other Classic Stories
author: Philip K. Dick
card_summary: A collection of Philip K. Dick's classic short stories, anchored by the title story "Second Variety." At 499 pages, the volume includes major stories such as The Cookie Lady, Beyond the Door, Jon's World, The Commuter, Breakfast at Twilight, The Hood Maker, Human Is, Adjustment Team, The Impossible Planet, Impostor, James P. Crow, Planet for Transients, Small Town, and Prominent Author, among many others. Represents Dick's extraordinary range and productivity in the short fiction form.

title: Selected Stories of Philip K. Dick
author: Philip K. Dick
card_summary: A selection of short stories by Philip K. Dick, likely a Pantheon or Houghton Mifflin Harcourt anthology. The text excerpt lists Dick's extensive novel bibliography rather than a table of contents, suggesting this is a standalone short story collection. Dick's novels listed span his entire career from Solar Lottery through The Transmigration of Timothy Archer, indicating this was published after 1982. The collection draws from his prolific short fiction output of the 1950s and 1960s.

title: The Book of Philip K. Dick (1973)
author: Philip K. Dick
card_summary: A 1973 short story collection by Philip K. Dick dedicated to 'Leslie Busby: the little black-haired girl I love so much.' The volume collects stories originally written between approximately 1952 and 1955, including Nanny, The Turning Wheel, The Defenders, Adjustment Team, PSI-Man, The Commuter, A Present for Pat, Breakfast at Twilight, and Shell Game. These early stories demonstrate Dick's developing voice and his characteristic preoccupation with suburban life, technology, and the nature of reality.

title: The Collected Stories of Philip K. Dick, Volumes 1-5
author: Philip K. Dick
card_summary: The complete five-volume Collected Stories of Philip K. Dick in a single omnibus edition, published by Underwood-Miller in 1987. The five volumes are: Vol. 1 Beyond Lies the Wub (preface by PKD, foreword by Steven Owen Godersky, introduction by Roger Zelazny), Vol. 2 Second Variety, Vol. 3 The Father-Thing, Vol. 4 The Days of Perky Pat, and Vol. 5 The Little Black Box. At 2,275 pages this is the definitive collection of Dick's short fiction across his entire career.

title: The Jungle People (Berkeley Daily Gazette, February 17, 1942)
author: Philip K. Dick
card_summary: An early Philip K. Dick short piece titled 'The Jungle People,' published in the Berkeley Daily Gazette on February 17, 1942. This is among the earliest known published writings by PKD, who would have been approximately thirteen years old at the time. It represents a significant archival item documenting PKD's juvenilia and earliest public writing efforts prior to his professional science fiction career.

title: The Little Movement
author: Philip K. Dick
card_summary: A short story titled 'The Little Movement' by Philip K. Dick. The story is a known PKD short story, likely from the early 1950s. 'The Little Movement' concerns toys that develop sentience and organize for survival, a theme characteristic of Dick's early work exploring the nature of consciousness and the boundaries between living and non-living things.

title: The Magician's Box (Berkeley Daily Gazette, February 7, 1944)
author: Philip K. Dick
card_summary: An early Philip K. Dick short piece titled 'The Magician's Box,' published in the Berkeley Daily Gazette on February 7, 1944. PKD would have been approximately fifteen years old at the time of publication. Like other Berkeley Daily Gazette juvenilia, this piece documents PKD's earliest public literary output and is of considerable biographical and archival significance for researchers of his formative years.

title: The Minority Report and Other Classic Stories (Collected Stories of Philip K. Dick, Volume 4)
author: Philip K. Dick
card_summary: Volume 4 of The Collected Stories of Philip K. Dick, centering on the well-known title story 'The Minority Report' and gathering Dick's short and medium-length fiction from 1954 to 1964, including some previously unpublished works. Stories in this volume include 'Service Call', 'Stand By', and 'The Days of Perky Pat', among others. The collection demonstrates Dick's prolific output during his peak short fiction period and showcases the range of speculative ideas that would later influence film and television adaptations.

title: The Philip K. Dick Reader
author: Philip K. Dick
card_summary: An anthology of short fiction by Philip K. Dick, opening with the story 'Fair Game', in which Professor Anthony Douglas returns home exhausted only to find his routine evening disrupted. The Reader collects a broad selection of Dick's shorter works and serves as an accessible entry point into his science fiction output. The volume gathers stories across different phases of his career, offering a representative sample of his recurring themes: suburban alienation, altered perception, and the instability of everyday reality.

title: The Philip K. Dick Reader
author: Philip K. Dick
card_summary: A major short story collection spanning Dick's career, at 505 pages. Described on the back cover with a Wall Street Journal quote praising Dick's ability to put readers inside people's minds. The Philip K. Dick Reader demonstrates the range that has led many readers to consider him the greatest science fiction mind on any planet. Published posthumously as interest in Dick's work continued to mount.

title: The Preserving Machine and Other Stories
author: Philip K. Dick
card_summary: A short story collection first published in 1969, anchored by the title story about a machine that transforms musical compositions into living creatures to preserve them. The collection showcases Dick's inventive short fiction from the 1950s and 1960s. Ace Books EPUB edition.

title: The Second Philip K. Dick MEGAPACK
author: Philip K. Dick
card_summary: An anthology of Philip K. Dick's short fiction published by Wildside Press in 2015 as part of their MEGAPACK series. Contains thirteen stories including 'The Unreconstructed M', 'Sales Pitch', 'Shell Game', 'Breakfast at Twilight', 'Adjustment Team', 'Foster, You're Dead', 'The Crawlers', 'The Golden Man', 'The Last of the Masters', 'The Turning Wheel', 'Upon the Dull Earth', 'Human Is', and 'Meddler'. Compiled as a companion to the first Philip K. Dick MEGAPACK volume, this collection emphasizes Dick's prolific short fiction output.

title: The Short Happy Life of the Brown Oxford and Other Classic Stories (Collected Stories of Philip K. Dick, Volume 1)
author: Philip K. Dick
card_summary: Volume 1 of The Collected Stories of Philip K. Dick, covering his earliest published short and medium-length fiction from 1952 to 1955, including some previously unpublished stories. Contains 25 or more stories including 'Beyond Lies the Wub', 'The Variable Man', 'Roog', 'The Skull', 'The Defenders', 'Paycheck', and the title story 'The Short Happy Life of the Brown Oxford'. Features a preface by Philip K. Dick, a foreword by Steven Owen Godersky, and an introduction by Roger Zelazny. Represents the formative phase of Dick's short fiction career.

title: The Visitation (Berkeley Daily Gazette, August 14, 1944)
author: Philip K. Dick
card_summary: An early Philip K. Dick short piece titled 'The Visitation,' published in the Berkeley Daily Gazette on August 14, 1944. PKD would have been approximately fifteen to sixteen years old at publication. This is part of a series of juvenilia contributions PKD made to the Berkeley Daily Gazette in the early 1940s and is an important archival item documenting his earliest creative output before his professional science fiction career began.
